methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $335M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

data.narrative:
Tokamak Energy, a Series C-stage nuclear energy company founded by David Kingham and Mikhail Gryaznevich, has raised $335M+ to date. The broader nuclear sector includes 3 public comparables (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private peers including Oklo, TerraPower, X-Energy, Kairos Power, and Nano Nuclear Energy.

A peer-median valuation approach was not applicable due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables within the nuclear energy sector. The narrow peer set lacks three or more directly comparable datapoints needed to establish a reliable median. Accordingly, the analysis relies on stage-progression methodology, which applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical of Series C advancement to the $335M total capital raised as a valuation proxy. This method yields an implied valuation of $570M.

The stage-progression method supports a valuation range of $484M (low) to $655M (high), with a median of $570M. The range reflects inherent uncertainty in applying standardized step-up multiples to a pre-revenue or nascent-revenue frontier-tech company. Key sensitivities include: (1) the actual revenue traction or technical milestones achieved since the last funding round, (2) capital efficiency relative to sector peers, and (3) technology risk and development timelines, none of which are fully captured by stage-based mechanics alone. An alternative peer-centric approach would require a thicker comparable set or broader sector sampling.
